title: Portfolio Rebalancing Strategy: When & How Often to Rebal...
description: Think rebalancing your portfolio every year is the smart move? Discover when sticking to a calendar actually hurts your returns and the threshold strategy the pros use instead.

schemas.headline: Portfolio Rebalancing: When, How Often, and Which Method Works Best
schemas.description: Think rebalancing your portfolio every year is the smart move? Discover when sticking to a calendar actually hurts your returns and the threshold strategy the pros use instead.

acceptedAnswer.text: Most advisors recommend reviewing your portfolio at least annually. However, deciding exactly how often to rebalance depends on your strategy: you can rebalance on a set calendar date (like quarterly or yearly) or when your asset allocation drifts beyond a certain threshold (like 5%).

mainEntity.name: What is the best portfolio rebalancing strategy?

acceptedAnswer.text: There is no single 'best' strategy, but threshold rebalancing (only trading when allocations drift 5% to 10% off target) often provides a better balance of risk control and lower transaction costs than strict calendar rebalancing.

mainEntity.name: What is calendar vs threshold rebalancing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Calendar rebalancing involves adjusting your portfolio back to its target allocation at fixed time intervals (e.g., every January 1st). Threshold rebalancing only triggers a trade when an asset class drifts outside a pre-set tolerance band (e.g., +/- 5% of its target weight), regardless of the date.

mainEntity.name: Does rebalancing a portfolio trigger taxes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you rebalance in a taxable brokerage account by selling profitable assets, you will trigger capital gains taxes. To minimize this, you can direct new contributions or dividends to underweight assets instead of selling.

mainEntity.name: When does portfolio rebalancing hurt returns?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rebalancing can actually hurt returns during strongly trending markets. By continuously selling your winners to buy losers, you cut your momentum short. Rebalancing works best in mean-reverting or volatile markets.
